event: Early modern humans and their predecessors inhabit the region, with stone tools and evidence of hunter-gatherer life found across what is now Mozambique.
Bantu-speaking peoples migrate into the region, introducing iron-working, agriculture, and settled village life that displaces earlier Khoisan hunter-gatherers.
Bantu agricultural communities expand across the Mozambican interior and coast, cultivating crops and raising livestock.
Swahili-influenced coastal trading settlements emerge, linking the coast to Indian Ocean commerce in gold, ivory, and slaves.
Sofala develops into a major Indian Ocean port, exporting gold from the interior to Arab, Persian, and Indian merchants.
Arab and Swahili traders establish settlements along the coast, including Ilha de Mozambique, integrating the region into Islamic trade networks.
The Kingdom of Mapungubwe to the southwest dominates regional gold trade, drawing wealth through Mozambican coastal ports.
The Kingdom of Zimbabwe rises, channeling interior gold and goods through the port of Sofala on the Mozambican coast.
The Mutapa (Monomotapa) Empire emerges in the Zambezi valley region, controlling gold trade routes to the coast.
Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama lands at Mozambique Island and other coastal points en route to India, marking the first European contact.
The Portuguese establish a fortified trading post at Sofala, beginning their domination of the coastal gold trade.
Portugal founds a settlement on Mozambique Island, which becomes the principal base and later capital of Portuguese East Africa.
The Portuguese establish trading stations at Sena and Tete on the Zambezi River to access interior gold and ivory.
The Mutapa ruler signs a treaty making the empire effectively a vassal of Portugal, expanding Portuguese influence inland.
Omani Arabs capture Mombasa from Portugal, pushing Portuguese control southward and consolidating it around Mozambique.
Portugal makes Mozambique a separate colony, no longer administered from Portuguese India, with its own captain-general.
Portugal builds the Fort of Sao Sebastiao and strengthens the slave trade, exporting captives to plantations across the Indian Ocean and the Americas.
The Nguni migrations from the south (Mfecane) bring the Gaza Empire under Soshangane, which comes to dominate much of southern Mozambique.
The Gaza Empire reaches its height, controlling territory between the Zambezi and Limpopo rivers and rivaling Portuguese power.
The Berlin Conference and the Scramble for Africa formalize European claims, with Portugal asserting control over Mozambique.
An Anglo-Portuguese treaty fixes Mozambique's borders, and chartered companies are granted authority to administer and exploit large territories.
Portuguese forces defeat and capture Gungunhana, the last emperor of Gaza, ending major African resistance and consolidating colonial rule.
Portugal moves the colonial capital from Mozambique Island to Lourenco Marques (later Maputo) in the developing south.
Portugal's Estado Novo dictatorship under Salazar tightens control, intensifying forced labor and the cash-crop economy in Mozambique.
Portugal reclassifies Mozambique as an overseas province to deflect growing international anti-colonial pressure.
The Mozambique Liberation Front (FRELIMO) is founded in Dar es Salaam under Eduardo Mondlane to fight for independence.
FRELIMO launches an armed guerrilla struggle against Portuguese colonial rule, beginning the War of Independence.
FRELIMO leader Eduardo Mondlane is assassinated by a parcel bomb, and Samora Machel later emerges as the movement's leader.
Portugal's Carnation Revolution topples the dictatorship, opening the way for negotiations on Mozambican independence.
Mozambique gains independence on 25 June with Samora Machel as president, and the country joins the United Nations.
The anti-communist insurgency RENAMO forms, backed by Rhodesia and later apartheid South Africa, igniting a devastating civil war.
FRELIMO declares itself a Marxist-Leninist party and nationalizes industry, while the civil war against RENAMO escalates.
Mozambique and South Africa sign the Nkomati Accord, in which each agrees to stop supporting the other's insurgents.
President Samora Machel dies in a plane crash near the South African border, and Joaquim Chissano succeeds him.
FRELIMO formally abandons Marxism-Leninism, moving toward a market economy and political pluralism.
A new constitution establishes multiparty democracy, a market economy, and renames the country the Republic of Mozambique.
The Rome General Peace Accords end the 15-year civil war that killed about a million people and displaced millions more.
Mozambique holds its first multiparty elections, with Joaquim Chissano and FRELIMO winning the presidency and parliament.
Mozambique joins the Commonwealth of Nations, the only member with no historical ties to the British Empire.
Catastrophic floods devastate central and southern Mozambique, killing hundreds and displacing hundreds of thousands.
Mozambique becomes a founding member of the African Union when it replaces the Organisation of African Unity.
Armando Guebuza wins the presidency for FRELIMO as Chissano steps down after voluntarily declining to seek a third term.
Major offshore natural gas discoveries in the Rovuma Basin promise to transform Mozambique into a significant energy exporter.
RENAMO renews low-level armed clashes with government forces, straining the fragile post-war peace.
Filipe Nyusi of FRELIMO takes office as president following the 2014 elections.
A hidden-debt scandal involving secret state-guaranteed loans triggers a financial crisis and suspension of donor support.
An Islamist insurgency erupts in the northern Cabo Delgado province, near major gas projects, displacing many thousands.
Cyclones Idai and Kenneth strike within weeks, causing catastrophic destruction, and the government and RENAMO sign a new peace deal.
Insurgents seize the town of Palma near gas facilities, prompting a TotalEnergies project halt and a regional military intervention.
Disputed elections won by FRELIMO's Daniel Chapo spark widespread protests and violent unrest across the country.
